title: Getting Started with Rust
excerpt: Restarting the Rust journey with a clean slate: installation, hello world, and Cargo basics.
subtitle: Starting again from scratch, this time with practice and small blogs to stay consistent.

sections.title: Why I'm Starting Over
paragraphs: Hey everyone! From today onward I'm starting Rust from scratch again. I had started earlier, stopped, and forgot a lot. This time I'm writing small blogs alongside my learning so I can pick up where I left off and actually retain the concepts.
The biggest mistake I made before was not practicing enough. Without practice, things fade and it takes time to get back into the zone. So now I'm committing to consistent practice and documentation.

sections.title: Learning Plan

paragraphs.value: . It can feel a bit theoretical at times, but I know it will teach me a lot of new concepts.

paragraphs: Along the way, I'll solve coding problems I've already done in other languages as well as new ones. I want to stay consistent this time, so let's start and see.
The introduction in the book explains who the book is for and how to use it. Prior coding experience helps you move faster, and without it some parts can feel harder at first.

You should see "Hello, world!" printed in the terminal.

sections.title: 3. Hello Cargo
paragraphs: Cargo is Rust's build system and package manager, similar to npm in Node or pip in Python.
For small programs you might not need dependencies, but as projects grow, Cargo makes it easy to manage packages and builds without compiling everything manually.

sections.title: Create a Cargo Project
paragraphs: Create a new project using Cargo:
This creates a hello_cargo directory with Cargo.toml, a .gitignore file, and src/main.rs.

sections.title: Project Structure
paragraphs: Cargo expects your source files inside the src directory. The top-level directory is for README files, license information, configuration files, and other metadata.

sections.title: Wrapping Up
paragraphs: That's all for today. See you tomorrow.

title: Variables in Rust
excerpt: Notes on variables, constants, and shadowing in Rust.
subtitle: Skipping the guessing game for now and focusing on Rust basics.

paragraphs.value: Hey everyone! Yesterday I skipped :( But I don't want to give any excuse or justification, so let's just start the next topic: Variables in Rust.
In the Rust Book, the second topic is
, but I'm skipping that for now. I'll cover the basics first and come back to it later.

sections.title: 1. Variables and Mutability

paragraphs.value: We can declare variables using the
keyword as shown in the code below.
The syntax for declaring variables is:
. We'll talk about variable types in upcoming blogs.

paragraphs: As shown in variable b's definition, the var_type is optional and determined by the compiler only because the value is initialized in the same statement.
If declaration and initialization are split like variable x, then var_type is mandatory, and the value can be given later. But we cannot use x before initialization as shown.

paragraphs.value: Variables are by default immutable in Rust. When a variable is immutable, once a value is bound to a name, you can't change that value. As shown in the code, if you try to assign a value to b or x you can't do it.

paragraphs: Below code shows how we can make variables mutable using the mut keyword in order to change the value that a variable holds.

sections.title: 2. Constants

parts.value: Like immutable variables, constants are not allowed to change, but there are a few differences between constants and variables.
First, we aren't allowed to use mut with constants. Constants aren't just immutable by default, they're always immutable. The var_type must be specified for constants.

paragraphs: Constants can be declared in any scope, including the global scope, which makes them useful for values that many parts of code need to know about.

parts.value: The last difference is that constants may be set only to a constant expression, not the result of a value that could only be computed at runtime. This means constants must be known at compile time.

paragraphs: Constants are valid for the entire time a program runs, within the scope in which they were declared.

sections.description: Using shadowing we can achieve the following things:

items.value: Change the value of an immutable variable
like x in the code above. This is safe because we explicitly do so.
Change the type of a variable
like characters, which is first a string then shadowed to characters.len() (a different type).
Change the mutability of a variable
like characters: first immutable, then mutable, then immutable again.
Shadow a variable in an inner scope without affecting the outer scope variable.
In the code above, the inner x is 12, and after the inner scope finishes, the outer x is still 6. Note that y is not shadowed, so its value changes in the outer scope.
